title: Why Pleasure Isn't Selfish: A Sexologist's Guide to Reclaiming Intimacy
guestBio: Dr. Holly Wood is a sexologist, sex therapist, and women's sexual wellness advocate dedicated to helping women reclaim their sexuality and dismantle shame. She specializes in helping women prioritize pleasure as part of a healthy, fulfilling life.
excerpt: Sexologist Dr. Holly Wood joins Aimee for a bold conversation about dismantling shame, reclaiming desire, and why pleasure is actually self-care. This is the conversation every woman needs to hear.
metaDescription: Sexologist Dr. Holly Wood shares how to reclaim intimacy, navigate desire in motherhood, and why pleasure is self-care, not selfish.
keyTakeaways: Pleasure is not selfish—it's an essential form of self-care that contributes to your overall well-being.
Motherhood naturally changes your relationship with your body and desire, and these changes are completely normal.
Open communication with your partner about your needs and boundaries is crucial for a fulfilling intimate life.
Understanding that desire works differently for women can help you approach intimacy without pressure or shame.
Creating safe spaces for pleasure in your relationship requires intentionality and ongoing conversation.
Dismantling shame around sexuality is essential for women's overall health and happiness.
Your sexuality is an integral part of who you are and deserves to be honored throughout all life stages.

pullQuotes.text: You have the right to pursue the sex and pleasure you desire
Reclaiming your connection to your body is a powerful, ongoing journey

timestamps.label: The Journey to Becoming a Sexologist
Motherhood's Impact on Body and Sexuality
The Importance of Self-Care and Pleasure

content:
Let's talk about something that might make you a little uncomfortable—and that's exactly why we need to talk about it. In a world where women are taught to give endlessly to everyone else, the idea of prioritizing pleasure can feel revolutionary, even selfish. But what if I told you that pleasure isn't selfish at all? What if it's actually one of the most important forms of self-care you can practice?

In this powerful episode of The Manage Her, I sit down with Dr. Holly Wood, a sexologist and women's sexual wellness expert, for a conversation that I wish I'd had years ago. We're diving deep into the topics that often get whispered about in hushed tones—intimacy, desire, sexuality, and how motherhood changes everything we thought we knew about our own bodies.

The Journey to Reclaiming Your Sexuality

Dr. Holly's path to becoming a sexologist wasn't traditional, but then again, nothing about reclaiming your sexuality in a world full of mixed messages is traditional. She shares how her own journey led her to dedicate her life to helping women dismantle the shame and guilt that so many of us carry around our desires and our bodies.

Here's what struck me most about our conversation: the realization that so many of the messages we've internalized about sexuality aren't actually serving us. From a young age, women receive conflicting information about their bodies and their desires. We're told to be desirable but not too sexual, to be available but not too eager, to prioritize everyone else's needs while somehow magically maintaining our own sense of self.

It's exhausting, and more importantly, it's damaging to our overall well-being. Dr. Holly explains how unlearning these messages isn't just about improving your sex life—it's about reclaiming your entire relationship with yourself.

Motherhood Changes Everything (And That's Okay)

If you're a mother, you know that having children changes your body in ways no one really prepares you for. But beyond the physical changes, motherhood fundamentally shifts how you relate to your own desires and needs. Dr. Holly normalizes what so many of us experience but rarely talk about: feeling disconnected from our bodies, struggling with desire, and wondering if we'll ever feel like ourselves again.

The truth is, your relationship with your body will evolve through different life stages, and that's not something to fix—it's something to honor. Dr. Holly offers practical advice for navigating intimacy during those challenging early motherhood years, reminding us that connection doesn't always have to look the same way it did before.

She gives permission for what so many of us need to hear: yes, quickies count. Yes, it's okay to prioritize efficiency sometimes. Yes, your changing body is still worthy of pleasure and connection. These aren't compromises—they're adaptations that honor where you are right now in your life.

Understanding How Desire Really Works

One of the most enlightening parts of our conversation centers around how desire actually functions for women. Dr. Holly explains that the cultural narrative around desire—that it should be spontaneous and constant—doesn't reflect the reality of how many women experience arousal and connection.

Understanding responsive desire versus spontaneous desire can be game-changing for your relationship with yourself and your partner. When you stop measuring your sexuality against unrealistic standards, you create space for pleasure to show up in ways that actually work for you.

This knowledge isn't just helpful—it's liberating. It removes the pressure and shame that can build up when your experience doesn't match what you think it should be. Dr. Holly emphasizes that there's no right or wrong way to experience desire, only your way.

Communication Is Everything

Throughout our conversation, Dr. Holly returns to one crucial theme: the absolute necessity of communicating with your partner about your needs, boundaries, and desires. This isn't always easy, especially when you're still figuring out what those needs are, but it's essential for creating the kind of intimate relationship you actually want.

She offers practical strategies for starting these conversations, even when they feel awkward or vulnerable. The goal isn't to have perfect communication from day one—it's to create an ongoing dialogue that evolves as you both grow and change.

Creating safe spaces for pleasure in your relationship requires intentionality. It means having conversations about what you need to feel connected, what barriers exist, and how you can work together to prioritize intimacy in ways that feel good for both of you.

Pleasure as Self-Care

Perhaps the most important message from Dr. Holly is this radical reframe: pleasure is not a luxury or an indulgence—it's self-care. When you prioritize your sexual well-being, you're not being selfish. You're taking care of an essential aspect of your health and happiness.

This perspective shift can be transformative. Instead of feeling guilty about wanting pleasure or connection, you can approach it as you would any other aspect of your wellness routine. Just as you prioritize sleep, nutrition, and exercise for your physical health, your sexual wellness deserves the same attention and care.

Dr. Holly reminds us that sexuality is an integral part of who we are as humans. It doesn't disappear when we become mothers or when life gets busy—it just needs to be honored and nurtured in new ways.

Permission to Evolve

What I love most about Dr. Holly's approach is that she gives women permission—permission to want pleasure, permission to communicate their needs, permission to evolve sexually throughout their lives, and permission to prioritize their own well-being.

Your sexuality at 25 doesn't have to look like your sexuality at 35 or 45. Your desires can change, your needs can evolve, and your relationship with pleasure can deepen over time. This isn't something to mourn—it's something to celebrate.

This conversation with Dr. Holly Wood is one of those episodes that I know will impact listeners long after the final minute. It's permission-giving, shame-busting, and ultimately empowering. Because when we reclaim our relationship with pleasure, we reclaim a fundamental part of ourselves.

You deserve pleasure. You deserve connection. You deserve to feel at home in your own body. And taking steps to honor these truths isn't selfish—it's essential.
